title: Formulation
text:
A hypergeometric random variable is a discrete random variable representing the number of successes observed when \(n\) items are drawn without replacement from a population of \(N\) items comprised of \(K\) successes and \(N - K\) failures. The quantities \(N\), \(K\), and \(n\) are the parameters of this distribution.
We use the notation \(X \sim \text{Hypergeometric}(N, K, n)\) to say that the value of random variable \(X\) follows a hypergeometric distribution with parameters \(N\), \(K\), and \(n\).
Its PMF (probability mass function, the function which returns \(P(X = x)\) for input value \(x\) of random variable \(X\)) is \[
f(x)=\frac{\binom{K}{x}\binom{N-K}{n-x}}{\binom{N}{n}}
\]
(BYUI Math Dept 112).
A hypergeometric distribution is ideal for modeling this system. We have a population (a production batch of lightbulbs, parameter \(N\)) with a known number of flagged outcomes (defective bulbs, parameter \(k\)) from which a random sample is drawn (parameter \(n\)) without replacement (meaning that each drawing is not independent of previous drawings).


The cumulative distribution function (CDF) \(F(X\leq x)\) for hypergeometric random variable \(X\) can be used, given that the number of defective bulbs in the batch is known to find the probability of there being more than \(x\) or fewer than \(x\) defective bulbs in a randomly selected batch. We’ll be able to use that to easily determine the false positive chance (chance that a sample passes given \(x\) defective bulbs in the batch) in testing. As may already be obvious, the false negative chance is always \(0\) because of the testing method. We’ll discuss that in section VII.



It’s easy to determine the chance of rejecting or passing a sample given that we know the amount of defective bulbs in the batch. But how do we find the chance of a batch containing \(x\) bad bulbs given that we pass it through testing or reject it? Recall that we made

title: Contextualization
text:
Project Assignment Sheet







In the case of a lightbulb factory which institutes randomized sample testing from batches of bulbs produced, how can we determine the relative impact on overhead costs of different test parameters and defect rates? We will answer three questions:

title: MATH 283 Unit 6 Group Project: Shedding a Light on Quality Control
text:
Resources

Adobe color wheel tool: https://color.adobe.com/create/color-wheel
MATH 423 READINGS AND EXERCISES, BYUI MATH DEPT 2021

title: Simplification
text:
Assumptions and Simplifications

A defective batch is a batch of 200 bulbs containing any defective bulbs.
The percentages of defects given in the problem statement reflect static proportions per batch. This means that a batch of 200 bulbs could contain exactly 2, 4, or 6 defective bulbs.
Random sampling. Test samples are chosen at random without replacement.
All counts are exact. This means that a sample of 20 always consists of exactly 20 bulbs, with no error tolerance.
We reduce the complexity of cost of poor quality (CPOQ) to an arbitrary \(1:100\) ratio. We suppose that a faulty bulb causes a loss equal to \(10000\%\) of its manufacturing cost.
WE’LL ASSUME IDEAL CONDITIONS AROUND THE TESTING PROCESS (NO HANDLING ERRORS, EQUIPMENT ERRORS, OR DIFFERENCES IN THE TESTING PROCESS BY BATCH SIZE).
We’ll assume parameters where none are given, and discuss their impact on cost and probabilties.

\(^*\) This Gemini conversation used to derive cost parameters for lightbulbs. As these estimates are generated by AI, exercise caution in using them as something other than proof-of-concept guesses for model formulation.
\(^{**}\) This article was used to estimate the maximum cost of bad bulb in terms of regular production cost.
